scenario: A team's BPMN tool defaults to horizontal swimlanes (left-to-right flow). They want to switch to vertical swimlanes (top-to-bottom flow). They wonder if BPMN supports this orientation.
question: Does BPMN support both horizontal and vertical swimlane orientation?

options.text: Only horizontal swimlane orientation is valid in BPMN -- partial; vertical orientation is also explicitly valid per the BPMN specification. The spec defines Pool orientation as an attribute that can be set to horizontal or vertical. Most BPMN tools default to horizontal because it aligns with left-to-right reading conventions common in Western cultures, but this is a tooling default, not a specification constraint. Teams working with top-to-bottom process flows or preferring vertical layouts can configure their BPMN editor to use vertical pools and lanes, which is fully compliant.

Yes -- BPMN supports both orientations. Horizontal (most common): Pool extends horizontally, Lanes are horizontal bands; sequence flows go left-to-right, which suits timeline-style processes. Vertical: Pool extends vertically, Lanes are vertical columns; sequence flows go top-to-bottom, which suits checklist or flow-chart style processes. Choice: cultural or tool preference; some teams prefer vertical for long processes with many sequential steps since it reduces horizontal scrolling. The orientation is purely a visual presentation concern -- Zeebe's execution engine ignores layout and runs the model regardless of diagram orientation. Verify your BPMN editor's support for vertical orientation before adopting it team-wide.

Vertical swimlane orientation is not allowed in BPMN -- wrong; the BPMN specification explicitly supports both horizontal and vertical orientations for Pools and Lanes. The isHorizontal attribute on the BPMN Diagram Interchange element controls orientation and defaults to true in most tools. Setting it to false produces vertical lanes. Runtime execution is completely unaffected by orientation -- Zeebe processes the BPMN model semantics, not the visual layout. If a modelling tool does not support vertical orientation, that is a tool limitation, not a BPMN specification constraint.

Orientation is defined individually per element within the diagram -- partial; orientation is typically set at the Pool level and applies uniformly to all Lanes within that Pool. The BPMN Diagram Interchange specification defines the isHorizontal attribute on the BPMNShape for Pools and Lanes, but for consistency and readability, teams normally set a single orientation for the entire Pool rather than mixing horizontal and vertical lanes within the same diagram. Per-element orientation switching creates confusing diagrams and is rarely a supported feature in standard BPMN editors.

a.text: Incorrect. BPMN spec allows both orientations: Horizontal swimlanes (most common): Pool: extends left-to-right; Lanes are horizontal bands stacked top-to-bottom. Flow direction: left-to-right primarily; activities chained horizontally. Reading: like timeline; left = earliest, right = latest. Vertical swimlanes: Pool: extends top-to-bottom; Lanes are vertical columns side-by-side. Flow direction: top-to-bottom primarily. Reading: like a checklist; top = first, bottom = last. Choice criteria: Process length: very long processes might be more readable vertically (fits screen height); short / parallel-heavy might be horizontal. Cultural preference: some regions / teams default to one or the other. Tool support: BPMN editors typically support both; verify your specific tool / version. At runtime: Orientation doesn't affect execution semantics. Zeebe runs the model regardless of visual layout. It's a presentation / readability choice. Mixing in collaboration diagrams: Multiple Pools could conceptually have different orientations, but readability usually favors consistency. For Camunda 8 + Web Modeler: verify the current version's UI support for vertical Pool orientation; horizontal is typically the default.

c.text: Incorrect. Option d) -- Partial. Whole-Pool level typically.

findings.message: option text contains its own verdict/rationale
